paper_title: Atmospheric Measurements using LIDAR and other techniques

methods.method_name: Polarization LIDAR Data Collection and Initial Processing
methods.description: Polarization LIDAR measurements were carried out on the campus of the Institute of Science and Technology (GIST) using the Depolarization LIDAR system (DPL) of the Korea Polar Research Institute (KOPRI) during May 2009. The DPL is equipped with a high-power (170 mJ), 10 Hz pulse repetition rate, Nd:YAG (532 nm) laser and an 8-inch Schmidt-Cassegrain telescope receiver. It was used with a fixed field of view (FOV) of 2 mrad. The temporal and spatial resolution of the DPL were 15 min and 2.5 m, respectively. The total linear volume depolarization ratio (δ) at 532 nm is obtained from the ratio of the total backscatter signals (aerosols and molecules), defined as δ = P s / (P p + P s), where P p and P s are the intensities of the parallel- and perpendicular-polarized backscatter signals. Calibration was performed by comparing δ measured under clear sky conditions to values from other LIDAR systems.

steps.description: Operate the Depolarization LIDAR system (DPL) at 532 nm wavelength with a 10 Hz pulse repetition rate, 170 mJ pulse energy, 2 mrad FOV, 15 min temporal resolution, and 2.5 m spatial resolution.
steps.searchable_summary: Operate LIDAR system to collect parallel and perpendicular backscatter signals.
inputs: Operating parameters (532 nm wavelength, 10 Hz pulse rate, 170 mJ energy, 2 mrad FOV, 15 min temporal resolution, 2.5 m spatial resolution)

steps.description: Calculate the total linear volume depolarization ratio (δ) profile at 532 nm from the ratio of the parallel- and perpendicular-polarized backscatter signals using the formula δ(z) = Ps(z) / (Pp(z) + Ps(z)).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate total linear volume depolarization ratio profile from raw LIDAR signals.
outputs: Total linear volume depolarization ratio profile (δ(z))

steps.description: Perform calibration of the depolarization measurement by comparing measured δ under clear sky conditions at a reference altitude (around 6 km) to values measured by other LIDAR systems or expected molecular values (e.g., 0.4% for molecular scattering).
inputs: Measured total linear volume depolarization ratio profile (δ(z)) under clear sky
Reference depolarization values (from other LIDARs or molecular theory)
outputs: Assessment of channel efficiency difference or cross-talk

methods.description: The aerosol depolarization ratio (δa) is calculated as an intensive parameter using an equation that relates it to the total linear volume depolarization ratio (δ), the molecular depolarization ratio (δm), and the height-dependent scattering ratio (R). The molecular depolarization ratio δm is taken as 0.004, accounting for the receiver-channel bandwidth. The scattering ratio R(z) is defined as (βa(z) + βm(z))/βm(z), where βa(z) and βm(z) are the aerosol and molecular backscatter coefficients, respectively.

steps.description: Obtain the total linear volume depolarization ratio profile δ(z).
inputs: Total linear volume depolarization ratio profile (δ(z)) from LIDAR measurements (Method 1, Step 2)
outputs: Total linear volume depolarization ratio profile (δ(z))

steps.description: Obtain the height-dependent scattering ratio profile R(z), calculated as (βa(z) + βm(z))/βm(z).
Define the molecular depolarization ratio δm as 0.004.
Calculate the aerosol depolarization ratio profile δa(z) using the formula: δa(z) = ((1 + δ(z)) * R(z) * δ(z) - (1 + δm) * δm) / ((1 + δ(z)) * R(z) - (1 + δm)).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate aerosol depolarization ratio profile using formula.
inputs: Total linear volume depolarization ratio profile (δ(z))

methods.method_name: Aerosol Extinction and Backscatter Coefficient Retrieval (Klett Method)
methods.description: The algorithm of Klett (1981, 1985) was used to retrieve the aerosol extinction and backscatter coefficients. This method requires assuming an extinction-to-backscatter ratio (lidar ratio). An iterative inversion approach was used to retrieve the appropriate values by tuning the lidar ratio based on the inter-comparison of aerosol optical depth (AOD) values derived from LIDAR and AERONET sun/sky radiometer data. The iteration continued until the difference between LIDAR-derived AOD and AERONET AOD was less than 10%.

steps.description: Obtain the raw or range-corrected LIDAR backscatter signal profile.
steps.searchable_summary: Get LIDAR backscatter signal profile for retrieval.
inputs: Raw parallel-polarized backscatter signal profile (Pp(z)) (from Method 1, Step 1)
Raw perpendicular-polarized backscatter signal profile (Ps(z)) (from Method 1, Step 1)
outputs: Raw or range-corrected total backscatter signal profile

steps.description: Obtain aerosol optical depth (AOD) data from AERONET sun/sky radiometer measurements for comparison.
outputs: AERONET AOD value(s) at relevant wavelength (e.g., 532 nm or convertible from 440 nm)

steps.description: Apply the Klett algorithm iteratively to the LIDAR backscatter profile, adjusting the assumed lidar ratio in each iteration. In each iteration, calculate the AOD from the retrieved extinction profile and compare it to the AERONET AOD. Continue iterating until the difference between the LIDAR-derived AOD and the AERONET AOD is less than 10%.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform iterative Klett inversion to retrieve aerosol extinction and backscatter profiles and determine lidar ratio.
inputs: Raw or range-corrected total LIDAR backscatter signal profile

methods.description: Column-integrated aerosol optical depth (τ) at 440 nm and the Ångström exponents (440-870 nm wavelength range, Å) were measured with the polarized-version of the AERONET 318-1 sun/sky radiometer at the LIDAR site. These parameters were retrieved using the standard AERONET algorithm. Daily-mean, level 2.0 data were used.

steps.description: Download daily-mean, level 2.0 AERONET data containing column-integrated aerosol optical depth (τ) at 440 nm and Ångström exponents (Å) from the AERONET website (http://aeronet.gsfc.nasa.gov).
outputs: AERONET data file (containing AOD at 440 nm, Ångström exponents)

methods.description: The daily number of pollen grains in the atmosphere was monitored by a Burkard 7-day-recording volumetric spore sampler. The sampler was situated on a rooftop 1 km away from the LIDAR site, placed at a height of 2 m above the rooftop and at an altitude of 85 m a.m.s.l.

steps.description: Operate a Burkard 7-day-recording volumetric spore sampler at the specified location and height.
steps.searchable_summary: Operate spore sampler to collect atmospheric pollen.
outputs: Raw pollen sample data (e.g., collection medium with deposited particles)

steps.description: Process the collected samples to count and record the daily number of pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: Count daily pollen grains from collected samples.

methods.description: Hourly meteorological data, including relative humidity, wind speed, temperature, and PM10 concentrations, were measured at the Gwangju Local Meteorological Administration. The observation height was 250 m a.m.s.l.

Radiosondes were launched four times a day (at 03:00, 09:00, 15:00, and 21:00 LT) by the Korean Meteorological Administration (KMA) at the Gwangju airport. The sondes provided vertically resolved information on temperature and potential temperature.

steps.description: Launch radiosondes four times daily at 03:00, 09:00, 15:00, and 21:00 LT.
steps.searchable_summary: Launch radiosondes to collect atmospheric profile data.
outputs: Raw radiosonde data stream (pressure, temperature, humidity, wind vs. altitude/time)

steps.description: Process raw radiosonde data to obtain vertical profiles of temperature and potential temperature.
steps.searchable_summary: Process raw radiosonde data into vertical profiles of temperature and potential temperature.
